narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 48.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use to ensure we admit students likely to succeed in their legal careers. This prediction draws from patterns in data about past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to forecast bar exam outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your school that year—far below the average of around the 6th decile for those who passed the bar. This low ranking stood out strongly to the model, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong performance over time, which has been a key predictor of bar success in similar cases. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 35, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, since it's reasonably competitive even if slightly under the 37.27 average for bar passers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 also helped your case a bit, nudging things toward success compared to the typical 3.24 for those who passed, showing some foundational strength. While your high family income and full-time study plans align well with successful students—high income is uncommon but present in about 8.5% of passers, and full-time enrollment is the norm at 93%—they weren't the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by having solid but not standout test scores and plans, overshadowed by that early and late low decile performance that typical admits avoid. The model saw your situation as riskier than the average accepted student, whose stronger year-end rankings often tip the scales toward bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it higher or strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, which could reshape your profile for future applications. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these academic decile gaps and reapplying to schools with more flexible thresholds. You're clearly motivated, with a strong undergraduate base and supportive background—focusing on those improvements could turn this around. Keep pursuing your legal goals; persistence pays off for determined applicants like you.
